content.text: CODEOWNERS defines who reviews code changes at merge time. On-call rotations define who is paged when those changes cause production incidents. In most engineering organisations these two lists are maintained separately — and they drift apart. For GenAI applications this drift is particularly costly: when a system prompt change causes a model hallucination at 2 AM, the on-call engineer needs to understand prompt engineering well enough to diagnose and roll back the change. If the on-call rotation is staffed by backend engineers who never reviewed the prompt and do not understand model behaviour, the mean time to recovery (MTTR) skyrockets.
Aligning CODEOWNERS with on-call rotations closes this gap:
the team that approved a change is the team paged when it fails

attrs.alt: On-call alignment: from CODEOWNERS to incident response

content.text: The misalignment between code owners and on-call responders typically emerges for three reasons:
On-call is staffed by seniority, not domain

— senior engineers rotate through on-call regardless of which codebase they know. A principal engineer from the backend team may be on-call the week after a prompt engineer merges a major system prompt rewrite.
CODEOWNERS is maintained by the platform team, on-call by SRE
— two different teams own these two artefacts with no process to keep them in sync.
AI artefacts are treated as config, not code
— prompt changes are often not considered 'real code changes', so on-call coverage is not updated when new AI teams take ownership of new paths.
: Pick any path in CODEOWNERS. Page the on-call engineer and ask them to diagnose a regression caused by a change to that path. If they cannot — CODEOWNERS and on-call are misaligned.
Alignment Mapping: From CODEOWNERS to On-Call

For each ownership domain in CODEOWNERS, there should be a corresponding on-call rotation staffed by members of the owning team. The mapping does not need to be one-to-one — a single rotation can cover multiple related domains — but every CODEOWNERS entry should trace to a rotation that can respond to incidents in that domain.

is a shared Slack channel or PagerDuty escalation policy that pulls in all AI domain on-call engineers simultaneously for incidents that span multiple services. Pre-configure this in your incident tool — do not try to assemble it manually during an active incident.

The safest way to prevent drift is to derive both CODEOWNERS entries and on-call routing from a single source of truth — the service catalog introduced in the ownership-boundaries sub-subtopic. When the catalog changes, a script re-generates both:
Run this script in a pre-commit hook or CI job whenever
changes. A PR that updates the catalog will automatically update both CODEOWNERS and PagerDuty routing in the same commit — eliminating the possibility of drift.
Runbook Integration: Linking Incidents to Code Owners
When an incident fires, the on-call engineer's first question is:
what changed recently, and who owns that area?
A runbook that embeds direct links to CODEOWNERS entries and recent PR history reduces the time to identify the responsible team and the responsible change.

# Runbook: GenAI Behaviour Regression

## Symptoms
- Model outputs have degraded quality, changed tone, or are returning unexpected content
- Eval scores dropped below baseline in the last monitoring window
- Users are reporting unexpected responses

## Step 1: Identify the affected domain
Run the blast-radius query against the affected service path:
```
python3 scripts/blast_radius.py <affected-path>
```
This prints the owning team and all consuming teams to notify.

## Step 2: Find recent changes
```bash
# List prompt changes in the last 48 hours
git log --since="48 hours ago" --oneline -- prompts/

# List tool schema changes
git log --since="48 hours ago" --oneline -- tools/schemas/

# See who owns the changed files (check CODEOWNERS)
cat .github/CODEOWNERS | grep "<path-prefix>"
```

## Step 3: Page the code owner
If the on-call engineer is not on the owning team, escalate to the
owning team's rotation immediately — do not attempt to diagnose
prompt or schema issues without domain expertise.

PagerDuty escalation: https://yourcompany.pagerduty.com/teams

## Step 4: Rollback procedure
```bash
# Identify the last good commit hash from eval metrics
python3 scripts/find_last_good_commit.py --service <service> --metric eval_score

# Revert the offending change
git revert <commit-hash> --no-edit
git push origin main
```

## Step 5: Post-mortem
File a post-mortem within 72 hours. Link the incident to:
- The PR that introduced the regression
- The CODEOWNERS entry that should have caught it
- Whether the owning team was on-call at the time
Run a periodic audit that checks whether every CODEOWNERS entry traces to an on-call rotation and whether that rotation is staffed by members of the owning team:
Gradual Alignment: Migrating Existing On-Call Rotations

If your current on-call rotation is not aligned with CODEOWNERS — for example, a single SRE rotation covers all services regardless of domain — migrate gradually rather than restructuring all rotations at once.
Map every CODEOWNERS entry to the nearest existing on-call rotation. Identify gaps where no rotation covers an AI domain.
Add AI domain owners as secondary responders to the existing rotation. They observe incidents without being primary.
Add AI domain owners as co-primary. Both SRE and domain owner are paged simultaneously.
Domain owner becomes primary responder. SRE becomes escalation path.
Run audit_oncall_alignment.py in CI to verify alignment is maintained.

The shadow phase is critical: AI domain engineers often have no incident response experience, and SRE engineers often have no AI behaviour expertise. Pairing them on real incidents for several weeks builds the cross-functional intuition both need.

Alignment quality is not binary — it exists on a spectrum from no alignment (the SRE on-call has never touched AI artefacts) to full alignment (every on-call engineer has reviewed changes in the path that caused the incident). Track these metrics to quantify and improve alignment over time:
% of incidents where the paged engineer owns the CODEOWNERS path that caused the incident
% of AI incidents that required escalation beyond the primary on-call
Median time to resolution segmented by CODEOWNERS domain
% of CODEOWNERS paths that have at least one on-call engineer who has reviewed a PR in that path in the last 90 days

For multi-service AI incidents — where a prompt change triggers a model behaviour change that causes a tool call failure that surfaces as an agent loop — no single on-call engineer has full context. A pre-configured
pulls in all relevant domain owners the moment the incident is escalated.

# infra/pagerduty-escalation-policies.yaml
# Defines the cross-team escalation chain for AI incidents.

escalation_policies:
  - name: all-ai-oncall-bridge
    description: "Cross-team escalation for incidents spanning multiple AI domains"
    escalation_rules:
      # Level 1: Page the primary on-call of all AI domains simultaneously
      - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 0
        targets:
          - type: schedule
            id: prompt-oncall
          - type: schedule
            id: tools-oncall
          - type: schedule
            id: platform-oncall
          - type: schedule
            id: eval-oncall
      # Level 2: If unacknowledged in 10 min, page tech leads directly
      - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 10
        targets:
          - type: user
            name: nlp-lead
          - type: user
            name: architecture-lead
      # Level 3: VP Engineering
      - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 30
        targets:
          - type: user
            name: vp-engineering

# Incident channel (pre-created in Slack)
incident_channels:
  ai-incident:
    topic: "Active AI incident bridge — all AI domain on-call join here"
    auto_invite_rotations:
      - prompt-oncall
      - tools-oncall
      - platform-oncall
      - eval-oncall
    runbook_link: "https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/genai-behaviour-regression"
you need them. Assembling these during an active incident costs minutes that translate directly to user-facing downtime.

On-call alignment is the operational counterpart to CODEOWNERS: code review ownership and incident response ownership must converge on the same team. For GenAI applications this alignment is not optional — the probabilistic, context-dependent nature of model behaviour means that diagnosing a production regression requires the same domain expertise as reviewing the code change that caused it.

Enforce alignment by deriving both CODEOWNERS and on-call routing from a single service catalog, running an automated audit in CI, and embedding CODEOWNERS lookups into incident runbooks so that even an out-of-domain on-call engineer can immediately identify and escalate to the right team.
